elements.sent-2411-001: In this note, we study the notion of random Dehn function and compute an asymptotic upper bound for finitely presented acylindrically hyperbolic groups whose Dehn function is at most polynomial with integer exponent.
elements.sent-2411-002: By showing that in these cases the random Dehn function is strictly smaller than the usual Dehn function we confirm Gromov's intuition albeit in a different model.
elements.sent-2411-003: Gromov's seminal program of classifying groups up to quasi-isometries has been a central problem in geometric group theory.
elements.sent-2411-004: Among the quasi-isometry invariants that have emerged, the Dehn function of a finitely presented group has played a central role.
elements.sent-2411-005: The Dehn function of a finitely generated group $G$ is a complexity measure of a direct attack of the word problem in $G$: it provides an upper bound on the area of a word of length at most $n$, that is, on the number of relations that must be applied to a word in the generators of $G$, in order to prove that it represents the trivial element in $G$; the bound is expressed as a function of the length of the word.
elements.sent-2411-006: Geometrically, it provides an upper bound on the area of filling discs for a loop of length at most $n$ in a simply connected Riemannian manifold on which $G$ acts geometrically; the bound is given in terms of the length of the loop.
elements.sent-2411-007: An outstanding feature of Dehn functions is that they characterize hyperbolic groups as precisely those finitely presented groups having subquadratic Dehn function \cite{gromov1992asymptotic,OlshanskiHyperbolic,BowditchSubquadratic .}
elements.sent-2411-008: The Dehn function of a finitely presented group, by definition, captures the worst-case complexity of the word problem in the group.
elements.sent-2411-009: However, for many algorithmic problems it is natural to inquire about the average-case complexity.
elements.sent-2411-010: Gromov defined \cite[5.A6.c]{gromov1992asymptotic the averaged Dehn function of a group $G$ as the average of the area over all words of length at most $n$ that represents the identity in $G$, and claimed that in several cases the asymptotic growth of the averaged Dehn function should be strictly smaller than that of the standard Dehn function.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Young \cite{young2008averaged confirmed Gromov's claim for the family of nilpotent groups.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Using another model of filling function, which we call random Dehn function, see \cref{def:randomdehn , we confirm Gromov's intuition by showing that for finitely presented acylindrically hyperbolic groups whose usual Dehn function is at most polynomial, the random Dehn function grows strictly slower than the usual Dehn function.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Roughly speaking, the random Dehn function of a group $G$ is defined as the expected values of areas in $G$.
elements.sent-2411-014: Intuitively, this is done by considering a random walk $(Z_n)_n$ on $G$, which we think as a path on $G$.
elements.sent-2411-015: We then join the extreme points of this path, using a (quasi-geodesic) combing $\alpha$ for $G$, to obtain a closed loop on $G$.
elements.sent-2411-016: The random Dehn function $R\delta_{(G, (Z_n), \alpha)}$ of $G$ is then defined by taking the expected values of areas of loops constructed in this way.
elements.sent-2411-017: Our main results, confirming Gromov's intuition, are the following, with the usual order on functions which we recall later.
elements.sent-2411-018: Let $(Z_n)_n$ be a simple random walk on $G$.
elements.sent-2411-019: For every quasi-geodesic combing $\alpha$ of $G$, we have that \begin{equation}R\delta_{(G,(Z_n), \alpha)}(n) \preccurlyeq \frac{n}{\log(n)}\delta_G(\log(n)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-021: Sisto shows that the random Dehn function of a hyperbolic group, relative to proper subgroups with at most polynomial Dehn funtion, is $\preccurlyeq n\delta(\log(n))$ where $\delta$ is the maximum of the Dehn functions of the peripheral subgroups.
elements.sent-2411-022: Random walks belong to the larger class of stochastic processes called tame quasi-homogeneous Markov chains, whose definition we recall in \cref{sec:preliminaries .}
elements.sent-2411-023: These process are invariant under quasi-isometries.
elements.sent-2411-024: In the case of tame Markov chains, we obtain the following result.
elements.sent-2411-026: Let $(w_n^\ast)$ be a tame Markov chain on $G$ and $\alpha$ any quasi-geodesic combing of $G$, then we have that \begin{equation}R\delta_{(G,(w^*_n), \alpha)}(n) \preccurlyeq \frac{n}{\log n}\delta_G(\log(n)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-028: If we consider these, then we obtain the following result.
elements.sent-2411-029: Let $(w_n^\ast)$ be a tame quasi-homogeneous Markov chain on $G$.
elements.sent-2411-030: Then, for any quasi-geodesic combing $\alpha$ of $G$ we have that: \begin{equation}R\delta_{(G,(w^*_n), \alpha)}(n) \preccurlyeq n \log n.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-031: Roughly, these inequalities state that the probability that the distance between the position of a random walk (or tame quasi-homogeneous Markov chain) after $k$ steps and a quasi-geodesic between the starting point and the position after $n$ steps is at least $t$ decays exponentially in $t$, see Theorem \ref{thm:small_devns for the exact statement.}
elements.sent-2411-032: We then use these inequalities to deduce that our sample path coming from the stochastic process, with high probability, stays close to a quasi-geodesic which is enough to bound the area of the filling and hence give an estimate for the random Dehn function.
elements.sent-2411-033: It is worth noting that another quasi-isometry invariant based on tame Markov chains has been considered by the second named author and A.
elements.sent-2411-035: Instead of looking at the Dehn function of generic points, there the authors look at the divergence of generic points.

elements.sent-2411-040: In this section, we recall some notions from geometric group theory as well as from the theory of random walks and Markov chains.
elements.sent-2411-041: We then define the notion of random Dehn function of a group.
elements.sent-2411-042: We recall the definition of an acylindrically hyperbolic groups, which is a generalisation of the notion of a hyperbolic group and we refer to \cite{Osin and the references therein for more properties and examples of this class of groups.}
elements.sent-2411-043: We say that a group acts acylindrically on a metric space $X$ if for every $R \geq 0$, there exist $N > 0$, $L > 0$ such that for every $x,y \in X$ with $d_X(x,y) \geq L$, we have \begin{equation}|\{g \in G \ | \ d_X(x,gx) \leq R, \ d_X(y,gy) \leq R \}| \leq N.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}|\{g \in G \ | \ d_X(x,gx) \leq R, \ d_X(y,gy) \leq R \}| \leq N.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-044: A group is acylindrically hyperbolic if it is non-virtually cyclic and acts acylindrically on a hyperbolic geodesic metric space with unbounded orbits.
elements.sent-2411-046: Non virtually cyclic relatively hyperbolic groups with proper peripheral subgroups;.
elements.sent-2411-047: All but a finite number of mapping class groups of connected oriented surfaces;.
elements.sent-2411-048: Non virtually cyclic groups acting properly on a CAT(0) space with a rank-1 element.
elements.sent-2411-049: We usually denote a Markov chain on a group by $(w_n^\ast)_n$, where $w_n^p$ denotes the position of the Markov chain starting at $p$ after $n$ steps and the quantity $\mathbb P [w_n^o = g]$ denotes the probability of getting from $o$ to $g$ in $n$ steps.
elements.sent-2411-050: We recall some definitions from \cite{GS21 and \cite{InducedQIs and refer to these for more details.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: For a finitely generated group $G$ with generating set $S$, with word metric $d_S$, we say that a Markov chain $(w_n^\ast)$ on $G$ is tame if it satisfies the following conditions:.
elements.sent-2411-052: Bounded jumps: There exists a $K>0$ such that for all $g \in G, n \in \mathbb N$ we have that $d_S(w_n^p, w^p_{n+1 ) \leq K$,.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Non-amenability: there exists $A>0$ and $\rho <1$ such that for all $g,h \in G$ and $n \geq 0$ we have $\mathbb P [w_n^g = h] \leq A\rho^n$,.
elements.sent-2411-054: Irreducibility: For each $s \in G$ there exists constants $\epsilon_s, K_s >0$ such that for all $g \in G$ we have $\mathbb P [w_k^g = gs] \geq \varepsilon_s$ for some $k \leq K_s$.
elements.sent-2411-055: For a bijection $f: G \rightarrow H$ and a Markov chain $(w^\ast_n)$ on $G$, there is a natural Markov chain in $H$ induced by $f$ called the push-forward Markov chain on $H$, which is denoted by $f_\#(w^p_n)$.
elements.sent-2411-056: This is the Markov chain such that $\mathbb P [f_\#(w^o_n)=h] = \mathbb P [w_n^o = f^{-1 (h)]$ for all $h \in H$, $o \in G$ and $n \geq 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Another property of Markov chains that we consider is the following.
elements.sent-2411-058: A Markov chain $(w^p_n)_n$ in $G$ is quasi-homogeneous if for some $D > 0$ it has the following property.
elements.sent-2411-059: For every $g,h \in G$ there is a bijection $f: G \rightarrow G$ such that $f(g) = h$ and for all $n \in \mathbb N$ and all $q \in G$ we have that $f_\#(w^q_n) = (w_n^{f(q) )$.}
elements.sent-2411-060: The following result shows that appropriate random walks, including simple random walks, are tame quasi-homogeneous Markov chains.
elements.sent-2411-061: Then $(z_n)_n$ is a tame quasi-homogeneous Markov chain.
elements.sent-2411-062: Let $S$ non-empty set and $\free S$ the free group on $S$.
elements.sent-2411-063: Let $G$ be a group and suppose it is given by a finite presentation $\calp = \langle S|R \rangle$.
elements.sent-2411-064: We denote by $|\cdot|_S$ the word-length with respect to $S$ and by $d_S$ the induced metric.
elements.sent-2411-065: We say that a word $w$ in $S$ is null-homotopic in $G$ if it represents the identity in $G$ and write $w =_\calp 1$.
elements.sent-2411-066: We define the area of a null-homotopic word $w$ in $G$ with respect to $\calp$ as the positive integer defined by \begin{equation}\area{\calp}{w} := \min \{ \ell \in N \mid w =_\calp \prod_{i = 1}^{\ell} a _{i} r _{i} ^{\epsilon _{i}} a _{i} ^{-1}, \ with \ a_i \in \free{S}, \ r_i \in R, \ and \ \epsilon_i \in \{\pm 1\} \}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\area{\calp}{w} := \min \{ \ell \in N \mid w =_\calp \prod_{i = 1}^{\ell} a _{i} r _{i} ^{\epsilon _{i}} a _{i} ^{-1}, \ with \ a_i \in \free{S}, \ r_i \in R, \ and \ \epsilon_i \in \{\pm 1\} \}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-067: The Dehn function of $\calp$ is the function $\delta_\calp: \N \rightarrow \N$ defined as \begin{equation}\delta_\calp (n) := \max\{\area{\calp}{w} \mid w =_\calp 1, \ |w|_S \leq n \}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-068: Changing the presentation of $G$ does not alter the asymptotic behaviour of the Dehn function of the presentations.
elements.sent-2411-069: Concretely, the Dehn function of $G$ is well-defined up to $\asymp$- equivalence of functions \footnote{Recall, two functions $f,g: [0, \infty) \rightarrow [0, \infty)$ are said to be $\asymp$-equivalent if $f \preccurlyeq g$ and $g \preccurlyeq f$, where $f \preccurlyeq g$ means that there exists constants $A,B >0$ and $C,D,E \geq 0$ such that $f(n) \leq Ag(Bn +C) + Dn +E$ for all $n \geq 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-070: One can show that the Dehn function of a groups is a well-defined quasi-isometry invariant up $\asymp$-equivalence (for details see \cite{AlonsoQI and \cite{BridsonHaefliger ).}}
elements.sent-2411-071: For a group $G$ generated by $S$, we can assume without loss of generality that $S$ is symmetric, namely that $S=S^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-072: There is a natural surjective map, called evaluation map, from the free monoid generated by $S$, denoted by $S^\ast$, to $G$ defined by sending each element in $S^\ast$, a word in $S$, into the element it represents in $G$.
elements.sent-2411-073: A morphism of monoids $\alpha: G \rightarrow S^\ast$ is called a combing for $G$ if it is a (set-theoretic) right inverse of the evaluation map.
elements.sent-2411-074: Geometrically, given $g \in G$ we can identify $\alpha(g)$ with a continuous path $\alpha_g \colon [0,l] \rightarrow \cay{G,S}$ connecting $1$ to $g$ which we call the combing line of $g$.
elements.sent-2411-075: In other words, a combing of $G$ is a choice of normal form for elements in $G$.
elements.sent-2411-077: We say that a combing $\alpha \colon G \rightarrow S^\ast$ is a $D$-quasi-geodesic combing if for every $g \in G$ the combing line $\alpha_g$ is a $(D,D)$-quasi-geodesic.
elements.sent-2411-078: We simply say that $\alpha$ is a quasi-geodesic combing if there is some $D \geq 1$ such that $\alpha$ is a $D$-quasi-geodesic combing.
elements.sent-2411-079: Let $G$ be a finitely presented groups with finite presentation $\calp = \langle S|R \rangle$ and $\alpha: G \rightarrow S^\ast$ be a combing for $G$.

elements.sent-2411-081: For the null-homotopic word in $S$ defined as $W_{x_1, \ldots, x_n = \alpha_1 \cdot \ldots \cdot \alpha_{n-1 \cdot \alpha_n^{-1}$ we denote \begin{equation}\Fill_{\calp, \alpha}(x_1, \ldots, x_n) = \area{\calp}{W_{x_1, \ldots, x_n}}.\end{equation}.}}

elements.sent-2411-084: The random Dehn function of $\calp$ with respect to $(w_n^\ast)$ and $\alpha$ is the function $R\delta_{(\calp,(w_n^\ast), \alpha) \colon \N \rightarrow \N$ defined as \begin{equation}R\delta_{(\calp,(w_n^\ast), \alpha)}(n) = \sup_{o \in G} \mathbb E [ \Fill_{\calp, \alpha}(x_{o,1}, \ldots, x_{o,n})].\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-085: The stochastic process $ (w^\ast_{n )_n$ that we consider is either a random walk driven by an admissible measure, or a tame Markov chain.}
elements.sent-2411-086: The reason for looking at tame quasi-homogeneous Markov chains instead of just random walks is that together with quasi-geodesic combings these make the notion of random Dehn function invariant under quasi-isometries, as we now show.

elements.sent-2411-152: In this note, we study the notion of random Dehn function and compute an asymptotic upper bound for finitely presented acylindrically hyperbolic groups whose Dehn function is at most polynomial.
elements.sent-2411-153: By showing that in these cases, if the group is not hyperbolic, then the random Dehn function is strictly smaller than the usual Dehn function we confirm Gromov's intuition albeit in a different model.
elements.sent-2411-154: In fact, we show that in these cases the random Dehn function is at most quadratic.
elements.sent-2411-155: An outstanding feature of Dehn functions is that they characterise hyperbolic groups as precisely those finitely presented groups having subquadratic Dehn function \cite{gromov1992asymptotic,OlshanskiHyperbolic,BowditchSubquadratic .}
elements.sent-2411-156: The Dehn function of a finitely presented group captures the worst-case complexity of the word problem in the group.
elements.sent-2411-157: Gromov defined \cite[5.A6.c]{gromov1992asymptotic the averaged Dehn function of a group $G$ as the average of the area over all words of length at most $n$ that represents the identity in $G$, and claimed that in several cases the asymptotic growth of the averaged Dehn function should be strictly smaller than that of the Dehn function.}
elements.sent-2411-158: Gromov's claim was first verified for finitely generated abelian groups by Bogopolski and Ventura \cite{BogopolskiVentura , and extended to all finitely generated nilpotent groups by Young \cite{young2008averaged .}}
elements.sent-2411-159: Using another model of filling function, which we call random Dehn function, see \cref{def:randomdehn , we confirm Gromov's intuition by showing that for finitely presented (non-hyperbolic) acylindrically hyperbolic groups whose usual Dehn function is at most polynomial, the random Dehn function grows strictly slower than the usual Dehn function.}
elements.sent-2411-160: For every quasi-geodesic combing $\alpha$ of $G$, we have that \begin{equation}R\delta_{(G,(Z_n), \alpha)}(n) \preccurlyeq \frac{n}{\log (n)}\delta_G(\log(n)) \preccurlyeq n^2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-161: In particular, if $G$ is hyperbolic, then $R\delta_{(G,(Z_n), \alpha) (n) \preccurlyeq n$.}
elements.sent-2411-162: Else, if $d=2$ then $R\delta_{(G,(Z_n), \alpha) (n) \preccurlyeq n \log n \prec \delta_G (n)$, and if $d>2$, then $R\delta_{(G,(Z_n), \alpha) \prec \delta_G$.}}
elements.sent-2411-163: Sisto shows that the random Dehn function of a hyperbolic group, relative to proper subgroups with at most polynomial Dehn function, is $\preccurlyeq n\delta(\log(n))$ where $\delta$ is the maximum of the Dehn functions of the peripheral subgroups.
elements.sent-2411-164: These processes are invariant under quasi-isometries.

elements.sent-2411-166: In particular, if $G$ is not hyperbolic, then $R\delta_{(G,(w^*_n), \alpha) (n) \preccurlyeq n \log n \prec \delta_G(n)$.}

elements.sent-2411-182: Hence, by the choice of $C_3$ we get \begin{equation}\mathbb P \left[ \exists i,j \leq n : \vert i-j\vert \geq C_3\log(n), \ d_X(w^{o}_i, w^{o}_j) \leq \frac{\vert i-j \vert}{C_3} \right] \leq \sum_{i=0}^n \left( \sum_{l \geq C_3\log(n)}K e^{-l/K} \right) \leq K n \cdot n^{-C_3/K} \sum_{t \geq 0} e^{-t/K} \leq C_3n^{-r}. \qedhere\end{equation}.

elements.formula-2411-033: \begin{equation} \e \Big[ \Fill_{\calp, \alpha}(o, \dots, w^{o}_n) \Big\vert \mathcal A_n \cap \mathcal B_n \Big] \leq \sum_{0 \leq i \leq t} \area{\calp}{P_{k_i}} \leq t \delta_G(300KC_1^2C_3D^2\log(n)) \leq \frac{D^2(Kn+1)}{98C_1^2\log(n)-D}\delta_G(300KC_1^2C_3D^2\log(n)). \end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-034: \begin{equation} \e \Big[ \Fill_{\calp, \alpha}(o, \dots, w^{o}_n) \Big] = \e \Big[ \Fill_{\calp, \alpha}(o, \dots, w^{o}_n) \Big\vert \mathcal A_n \cap \mathcal B_n \Big]\p[\mathcal A_n \cap \mathcal B_n]+\e \Big[ \Fill_{\calp, \alpha}(o, \dots, w^{o}_n)\Big\vert \mathcal A_n^C \cup \mathcal B_n^C \Big]\p[\mathcal A_n^C \cup \mathcal B_n^C] \leq \frac{D^2(Kn+1)}{98C_1^2\log(n)-D}\delta_G(300KC_1^2C_3D^2\log(n)) + ((K+D)n+D)^d(C_1n^{1-C_1}+n^{-d-1}) \leq C\Big(\frac{n}{\log(n)}\delta_G(\log(n))\Big)+Cn+C \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-196: Therefore, \begin{equation}\label{eq:general-bound-random} R\delta_{(G,(w_n^*), \alpha)}(n) \preccurlyeq \frac{n}{\log n} \delta_G(\log n)\end{equation} By hypothesis, we have that $\delta_G(n) \preccurlyeq n^d$.
elements.sent-2411-197: Since $(\log n)^\alpha \preccurlyeq n$ for $\alpha >0$, it follows from \eqref{eq:general-bound-random that \begin{equation}R\delta_{(G,(w_n^*), \alpha)}(n) \preccurlyeq n (\log n)^{d-1} \preccurlyeq n^2\end{equation} In particular, if $G$ is hyperbolic, that is if $d=1$, then we have that $R\delta_{(G,(w_n^*), \alpha) (n) \preccurlyeq n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-198: Whereas, if $G$ is not hyperbolic and $\delta_G(n) \asymp n^2$, then \begin{equation}R\delta_{(G,(w_n^*), \alpha)}(n) \preccurlyeq n \log n \prec \delta_G(n);\end{equation} if $G$ is not hyperbolic and $n^2 \prec \delta_G(n) \preccurlyeq n^d$, then $R\delta_{(G,(w_n^*), \alpha) (n) \prec \delta_G$.}
elements.sent-2411-199: As a consequence of \cref{thm:ah_rdehn we obtain the main results stated in the introduction, which we state again for the reader's convenience.}
elements.sent-2411-200: For every quasi-geodesic combing $\alpha$ of $G$, we have that \begin{equation}R\delta_{(G,(Z_n), \alpha)}(n) \preccurlyeq n^2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-201: Furthermore, if $G$ is hyperbolic, then $R\delta_{(G,(Z_n), \alpha) (n) \preccurlyeq n$.}
elements.sent-2411-202: Then, for any quasi-geodesic combing $\alpha$ of $G$ we have that: \begin{equation}R\delta_{(G,(w^*_n), \alpha)}(n) \preccurlyeq \frac{n}{\log(n)}\delta_G(\log(n)) \preccurlyeq n^2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-203: This follows from Theorem \ref{thm:small_devns as all these groups satisfy either \cite[Assumption 1 or Assumption 2]{GS21 and hence we can apply Theorem \ref{thm:ah_rdehn by noting that all acylindrically hyperbolic $3$-manifold groups have at most quadratic Dehn function (by the fact that they have an automatic structure \cite[Theorem 12.4.7]{epstein as they do not contain $Sol$ or $Nil$ components in their geometric decomposition), to conclude.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-204: Then, for any quasi-geodesic combing $\alpha$ of $G$ we have that: \begin{equation}R\delta_{(G,(w^*_n), \alpha)}(n) \preccurlyeq \frac{n}{\log(n)} \delta_G(\log(n)) \preccurlyeq n^2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-205: Hence, applying Theorem \ref{thm:ah_rdehn and using the fact that hierarchically hyperbolic groups have at most quadratic Dehn function \cite[Corollary 7.5]{BHS1 concludes the proof.}}
